claim: On the night of April 14, 1865 — five days after Confederate General Robert E. Lee's surrender at Appomattox Court House — President Abraham Lincoln was shot at Ford's Theatre in Washington, D.C. by stage actor John Wilkes Booth, who fired a single round from a .44-caliber Philadelphia Deringer into the back of Lincoln's head from the rear of the Presidential Box. Lincoln died at 7:22 a.m. on April 15 in the Petersen House across the street. Booth fled on horseback, was located eleven days later at Garrett's farm in Virginia, and was shot and killed by U.S. cavalry on April 26 before he could be brought to trial. Eight co-conspirators were tried by military commission; four (Mary Surratt, Lewis Powell, David Herold, and George Atzerodt) were hanged on July 7, 1865. The Deringer Booth used was recovered on the floor of the Presidential Box that night by theatregoer William T. Kent and is held today at Ford's Theatre. Simon Wolf, a Washington lawyer, B'nai B'rith member, and friend of Lincoln, wrote in his 1918 memoir Presidents I Have Known that he encountered Booth in front of the Metropolitan Hotel on the morning of April 14, 1865 — Booth having just come from calling on a senator's daughter at the National Hotel — and had drinks with him there; Wolf called it the last time he ever saw Booth. Wolf also wrote that he closely resembled Booth in feature ("After the tragedy I was compelled to remain in my house until after Booth's capture, for unfortunately I resembled him very much in feature") and that the historical painter Theodore Kaufmann later asked him to sit as Booth for Kaufmann's painting of the assassination. None of these Wolf details figure in the official 1865 conspiracy-trial record; the connection is documented only in Wolf's own primary account.

sources.label: U.S. Library of Congress — Lincoln Assassination collection, including transcripts of the 1865 Military Commission proceedings and the recovered evidence catalog.
Ford's Theatre — “Booth's Deringer,” official documentation of the murder weapon held in the Ford's Theatre National Historic Site museum collection.
U.S. National Archives — Records of the Office of the Judge Advocate General (Army), Case Files of Investigations by the Bureau of Military Justice, 1865 (Lincoln conspiracy trial records).

Simon Wolf, Presidents I Have Known From 1860 to 1918 (Press of Byron S. Adams, Washington, 1918) — Wolf's primary account of meeting Booth at the Metropolitan Hotel on the morning of April 14, 1865, of his resemblance to Booth, and of being asked by painter Theodore Kaufmann to sit as Booth for the assassination painting.
sources.quote: After the tragedy I was compelled to remain in my house until after Booth's capture, for unfortunately I resembled him very much in feature.

sources.label: Shapell Manuscript Foundation — “Simon Wolf: Patriot, Activist, and Statesman.” Secondary biographical profile that compiles Wolf's Booth-related episodes (resemblance, the Kaufmann painting, the morning meeting) with citations back to Presidents I Have Known.

notes: The Library of Congress and National Archives hold the canonical primary record (trial transcripts, witness depositions, evidence catalog). Ford's Theatre is the canonical museum custodian for the physical evidence (the Deringer, Booth's diary, the bullet itself is at the National Museum of Health and Medicine). The Wolf-Booth meeting, the resemblance, and the Kaufmann-painting episode are documented in Wolf's own 1918 memoir Presidents I Have Known. An earlier version of this record placed the meeting at the Willard Hotel; Wolf's memoir locates it at the Metropolitan Hotel and that correction is now reflected here. Downstream commentary treats the Wolf material as evidence of a larger conspiracy, but the mainstream historical record locates the conspiracy at the eight co-defendants who were tried in 1865.
